sources.WDI: World Bank World Development Indicators, https://data.worldbank.org
sources.NBS: Nigeria National Bureau of Statistics, Labour Force Survey 2022 Q4

data_gaps: Informal sector wage data: NBS Labour Force Survey 2022 provides partial coverage; urban informal wages poorly tracked
Occupation-level wage data: Not available at ISCO-08 disaggregation for Nigeria
Youth NEET rate: NBS LFS 2022 estimate; significant measurement uncertainty
Regional variation: National figures mask major Lagos/Abuja vs. rural North divergence

gdp_per_capita_usd.note: Sharp decline from 2014 peak of $3222 due to oil price crash and naira depreciation

population_millions.note: Largest population in Africa. Adding ~5 million people per year

unemployment_rate_pct.note: Nigeria changed its unemployment definition in 2022. Pre-2022 rate was ~33% under old NBS methodology. Not comparable across years.

youth_neet_pct.note: Significantly higher than Ghana. Reflects large northern states with low female education participation

informal_employment_pct.note: One of the highest informal employment shares globally

female_labour_participation_pct.note: Lower than Ghana; reflects lower participation in northern states

wages._note: Formal sector wage data only. Informal sector wages not systematically tracked at national level.

mean_monthly_earnings_usd._note: Converted from NGN at 2022 parallel market rate (~700 NGN/USD). Official rate understates real purchasing power loss.

all_sectors.source: NBS Labour Force Survey 2022; ILO ILOSTAT partial

information_communication.source: NBS LFS 2022; Lagos tech sector premium

minimum_wage_usd_monthly.source: Nigeria NMW NGN 30,000/month at ~480 NGN/USD (2023 official rate)
minimum_wage_usd_monthly.note: Minimum wage rarely enforced in informal sector. Increased to NGN 70,000 in 2024 under new administration.

primary_completion_rate_pct.note: Well below Ghana. Significant regional disparity — northern states average ~40%

human_capital_index.note: Significantly below Ghana (0.45). Large gap between Lagos/Abuja and north.

mobile_money_adoption_pct_adults.note: Lower than Ghana due to bank-centric fintech regulation (CBN policies restricted mobile money until 2022)

sector_growth_outlook._source: World Bank Nigeria Economic Update 2023; ILO Nigeria Country Brief; PwC Nigeria Economic Outlook 2024
sector_growth_outlook._note: Second econometric signal — sector employment growth trajectory

sectors_growing.rationale: Lagos tech ecosystem (Yaba, Lekki) — fastest growing in SSA. Andela, Flutterwave, Paystack effect.
Post-CBN liberalisation: OPay, PalmPay, Moniepoint growing agent networks rapidly
Afrobeats global expansion; streaming revenue; formal employment growth in content
Jumia, Konga, informal WhatsApp commerce expansion
Population growth driving demand; significant undersupply

sectors_at_risk.rationale: Automation of routine office tasks. BPO sector under pressure.
ATM and mobile banking reducing teller demand

wittgenstein_projections._source: Wittgenstein Centre for Demography and Global Human Capital (2018), Education projection v.2.0
wittgenstein_projections._note: Education level distribution for Nigeria, ages 15-29, 2025 vs 2035

ages_15_29.key_trend: Nigeria has much larger no-education and primary-only population than Ghana. By 2035, meaningful improvement projected but slower than Ghana trajectory. Massive youth cohort means absolute numbers of out-of-school youth remain high even as percentages improve.
